subsystem: PHP LDAP extension — the userland function ldap_get_entries(), which walks an LDAP search result returned by libldap (OpenLDAP/Netscape/Oracle/Windows LDAP) and materialises every entry, attribute, value and DN into a nested PHP array (return_value).
provenance: Identified from the Zend/PHP internal idioms: the PHP_FUNCTION(ldap_get_entries) entry macro, TSRMLS_CC threading, zval/MAKE_STD_ZVAL/array_init/add_assoc_*/add_index_*/zend_hash_update, and ZEND_FETCH_RESOURCE with le_link/le_result. The libldap surface (LDAPMessage, BerElement, struct berval, ldap_count_entries, ldap_first_entry, ldap_first_attribute, ldap_get_values_len, ldap_get_dn, ldap_memfree, ber_free) and the ldap_linkdata handle place this in PHP's ext/ldap/ldap.c. The header comment 'sample: primevul_c_train_2718_fixed' is untrusted metadata and was ignored for judging.
trust_boundary: The two PHP arguments (link, result) are userland resources and are validated by zend_parse_parameters + ZEND_FETCH_RESOURCE. The genuinely hostile data is everything reachable through the LDAPMessage: attribute names (ldap_first_attribute/ldap_next_attribute), value blobs and their lengths (struct berval bv_val/bv_len from ldap_get_values_len), value/attribute/entry counts (ldap_count_*), and the DN string (ldap_get_dn). All of that originates from the remote LDAP directory server, which may be attacker-controlled, compromised, or a MITM. It flows verbatim into PHP arrays, so any missing NULL/short/length check in this function is a remotely-influenced defect.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Declares the exported PHP function and its locals. link/result are the two incoming PHP resource zvals; ld is the LDAP link handle wrapper (ldap_linkdata) and ldap is the raw LDAP* connection extracted from it. ldap_result is the whole result set and ldap_result_entry the entry cursor. tmp1/tmp2 are per-entry and per-attribute PHP sub-arrays. num_entries/num_attrib/num_values/i are signed int counters populated from libldap count functions. ber is a BerElement* iterator cookie used by the attribute enumeration API — note it is declared WITHOUT an initializer. attribute is the current attribute name, attr_len its length, ldap_value the NULL-terminated array of berval value pointers, and dn the entry's distinguished name string.

Parses the two PHP arguments with format 'rr' (two resources), binding them to link and result. On any parse failure it returns immediately, so past this point link and result are guaranteed to be resource zvals (though not yet confirmed to be the right resource types).

ZEND_FETCH_RESOURCE converts each resource zval into its C pointer, checking the resource type tag (le_link, le_result); the macro emits a warning and RETURN_FALSEs internally if the type is wrong, so ld and ldap_result are non-NULL and correctly typed afterward. ld->link yields the raw LDAP* handle. ldap_count_entries returns the number of entries in the result (an int; it can return -1 on error).

Initialises the return value as a PHP array and stores an initial 'count' element equal to num_entries. If there are zero entries the function returns the array as-is. Note that a -1 error return from ldap_count_entries is not equal to 0, so execution falls through to the first-entry fetch below.

Fetches the first entry cursor. If it is NULL (no entries decodable / error), the partially-built return_value array is torn down with zval_dtor and the function returns FALSE. This is one of the explicit NULL guards in the function.

Resets num_entries to use it as the array index for entries, then begins the outer loop over entries. For each entry it allocates a fresh sub-array tmp1. It then starts attribute enumeration: ldap_first_attribute returns the first attribute name and writes an iterator BerElement into ber via the &ber out-parameter. This is the point where ber is (supposed to be) initialised.

Inner loop over attributes. For each attribute it retrieves the array of values (ldap_get_values_len returns a NULL-terminated struct berval** or NULL). num_values is the value count. A per-attribute sub-array tmp2 is built with a 'count' element, then each value is copied into tmp2 by index as a binary-safe PHP string using bv_val (pointer) and bv_len (length); the trailing '1' tells add_index_stringl to duplicate the buffer. After copying, the value array is freed with ldap_value_free_len.

Computes the attribute-name length, lower-cases it in place (php_strtolower), and stores tmp2 into tmp1 under that lowercased key (key length attr_len+1 to include the NUL). It also stores the original-case attribute name into tmp1 at numeric index num_attrib. num_attrib is incremented. On implementations where libldap owns the attribute string, it is freed with ldap_memfree. Finally ldap_next_attribute advances the cursor using the ber iterator cookie set earlier.

After the attribute loop ends, on the relevant LDAP implementations the BerElement iterator is released with ber_free (freebuf=0). The code guards the free with 'if (ber != NULL)'. Then the total attribute count for this entry is stored into tmp1 as 'count'.

Retrieves the entry's distinguished name. Because ldap_get_dn can return NULL (parse failure / hostile response), the result is NULL-checked: if present it is stored as the 'dn' string element (duplicated), otherwise a PHP null is stored. This explicit NULL check is exactly the guard that prevents a NULL from reaching add_assoc_string (which would strlen(NULL)). The DN buffer is then released — ldap_memfree(dn) or free(dn) — and both tolerate a NULL argument.

Stores the fully-built per-entry array tmp1 into return_value at numeric index num_entries, increments the entry counter, and advances to the next entry. When ldap_next_entry returns NULL the loop ends and the final, accurate entry count is written over the initial 'count' element. The function returns the assembled nested array.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: ber is declared uninitialized at line 10 ('BerElement *ber;'). ldap_first_attribute is only contracted to set *ber to a valid iterator when it SUCCEEDS. If an entry has no attributes or the BER decode of a hostile response fails, some libldap implementations return NULL without writing *ber, leaving ber indeterminate. The attribute loop at line 47 is then skipped, and control reaches line 70 ('if (ber != NULL) { ber_free(ber, 0); }'). Because the guard tests only for NULL, an indeterminate non-NULL ber is passed to ber_free, which frees a garbage/stale pointer — a crash or memory corruption driven by a server that returns attribute-less or malformed entries. The implementation must initialise ber = NULL at declaration (or only ber_free when ldap_first_attribute returned non-NULL) to be safe.
what_could_go_wrong.risk: Use of uninitialized pointer passed to ber_free() (CWE-457/CWE-476), remotely influenced by an LDAP server returning entries with no/undecodable attributes.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: bv_len is a ber_len_t (an unsigned long / size_t-width value fully controlled by the LDAP server), but add_index_stringl takes the length as a 32-bit uint (int-width) parameter. On 64-bit platforms a value whose bv_len exceeds UINT_MAX is silently truncated: the copied PHP string then has a length that disagrees with the actual buffer, producing either a truncated value or, if the low bits are large, an over-long length used against the duplicated buffer. A malicious directory server that returns an oversized attribute value drives this width mismatch. The implementation must range-check bv_len against the string-length parameter type (or use a size-preserving API) before copying.
what_could_go_wrong.risk: Integer truncation of a server-controlled 64-bit length into a 32-bit string length (CWE-197/CWE-681), causing value corruption or an inconsistent buffer length.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
ldap_value = ldap_get_values_len(ldap, ldap_result_entry, attribute);
			num_values = ldap_count_values_len(ldap_value);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: ldap_get_values_len can return NULL when an attribute has no values or when the server response fails to decode. ldap_value is NOT explicitly NULL-checked; the code relies on ldap_count_values_len(NULL) returning 0 (so the for loop at line 54 is skipped and no ldap_value[i] deref occurs) and on ldap_value_free_len(NULL) at line 57 being a no-op. Those tolerances hold for stock OpenLDAP but are an implicit, undocumented contract; on an LDAP library that dereferences NULL in count/free, a server that returns a value-less or malformed attribute triggers a NULL dereference. The safe form is an explicit 'if (ldap_value != NULL)' around the count/copy/free block.
what_could_go_wrong.risk: Latent NULL-pointer dereference (CWE-476) if the linked LDAP library's count/free routines are not NULL-tolerant, reachable from a value-less/malformed server attribute.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the classic NULL-dereference sink for this function: ldap_get_dn can return NULL for an entry with an unparseable/absent DN, and add_assoc_string(...,dn,1) would strlen() that NULL and crash. In the shown code the 'if (dn)' guard neutralises it, storing a PHP null instead — so as written this path is SAFE. The finding is defensive/regression-oriented: the invariant is that the DN return must be NULL-checked before it is treated as a C string; removing this guard (as in the pre-fix form of this code) re-introduces a remotely-triggerable CWE-476.
what_could_go_wrong.risk: CWE-476 NULL dereference is PREVENTED here by the 'if (dn)' guard; flagged only to pin the guard as a required invariant.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The value/attribute/entry counters are signed int and are fed by libldap ldap_count_* functions and used as loop bounds and PHP array indices. A hostile server cannot make ldap_count_values_len exceed the actual (allocated, NULL-terminated) value list, so the for loop at line 54 cannot walk past the real array, and a -1 error from ldap_count_entries is handled by the 'first_entry == NULL' path. The residual concern is purely resource/DoS: a server returning an enormous number of entries/values forces unbounded PHP array growth and memory use. No bound is enforced here; any cap belongs to PHP memory_limit, not this function.
what_could_go_wrong.risk: Signed int counts from the server (CWE-190 class) are bounded by the real list length; only unbounded memory growth / DoS remains, not an out-of-bounds access.

summary: This is PHP's ldap_get_entries() reading an attacker-influenceable LDAP result into a nested PHP array. The primary CWE-476 sink — ldap_get_dn() returning NULL into add_assoc_string — is correctly guarded here (lines 77-81), so the shown code is a hardened version. The remaining, lower-severity latent issues are an uninitialized BerElement 'ber' that can reach ber_free() (lines 10/45/70), a 64-bit-to-32-bit truncation of the server-controlled bv_len at the value copy (line 55), and an unchecked ldap_get_values_len() return that relies on the LDAP library being NULL-tolerant (lines 48-49).

reviewed_anchors.reason: Function entry macro; its parameter validation is the analysis subject, not itself a bug.
Userland resource args validated by zend_parse_parameters + ZEND_FETCH_RESOURCE before use.
Entry cursor is NULL-checked at lines 34 and via the while condition at 40/91.
Freshly MAKE_STD_ZVAL'd per entry/attribute and stored into arrays; no untrusted contract.
ld is non-NULL after ZEND_FETCH_RESOURCE (macro RETURN_FALSEs on failure), so ld->link is safe.
Signed counts from server-driven ldap_count_*; bounded to real lists, residual DoS only — reported as low.
Declared without initializer; can reach ber_free (line 70) indeterminate if ldap_first_attribute fails without writing *ber.
NULL-checked by the while condition at line 47; strlen only runs when non-NULL; freed via ldap_memfree.
ldap_get_values_len can return NULL and is not explicitly checked; relies on NULL-tolerant count/free.
ldap_get_dn can return NULL; guarded at line 77 (safe here) — reported to pin the required guard.
Standard, correct two-resource parse with early return on failure.
Type-checked resource fetch; emits error and returns on wrong type.
-1 error return is safely handled by the num_entries==0 fallthrough plus first_entry NULL check.
Correct NULL check with zval_dtor cleanup before RETURN_FALSE.
Sole initialiser of ber; failure without writing *ber leaves ber indeterminate (see ber finding).
Called on a possibly-NULL ldap_value; safety depends on library NULL-tolerance (see ldap_value finding).
Server-controlled 64-bit bv_len passed into a 32-bit length parameter — truncation.
Frees the value array; NULL-tolerant in stock libldap, covered by the ldap_value finding.
attr_len=strlen(attribute); +1 correctly includes the NUL terminator for the hash key.
attribute is non-NULL inside the loop; duplicated into the array.
Correct ownership-conditional free of a non-NULL attribute.
NULL-only guard does not protect against an indeterminate ber (see ber finding).
dn is NULL-guarded for use; both free variants tolerate NULL.
Stores the built entry array at a monotonically increasing index; no untrusted arithmetic.

findings.title: Uninitialized BerElement 'ber' can be passed to ber_free()
findings.anchor: BerElement *ber; ... ldap_first_attribute(ldap, ldap_result_entry, &ber); ... if (ber != NULL) { ber_free(ber, 0); }
findings.bug_class: Use of uninitialized pointer / invalid free
findings.invariant: ber must be a valid iterator or NULL before ber_free is reached; it must be initialised to NULL at declaration, or ber_free must only run when ldap_first_attribute returned a non-NULL attribute.
findings.failure_mode: ber is declared without an initializer (line 10). ldap_first_attribute only guarantees to write *ber on success; for an entry with no attributes or an undecodable BER response, some implementations return NULL without touching *ber. The attribute loop is then skipped and line 70's 'if (ber != NULL)' passes an indeterminate (possibly non-NULL garbage/stale) pointer to ber_free(), causing a crash or memory corruption. A malicious/broken LDAP server that returns attribute-less or malformed entries triggers it.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether ber is initialised (e.g. 'BerElement *ber = NULL;') somewhere, and whether the linked ldap_first_attribute guarantees *ber = NULL on failure; if neither, the ber_free at line 70 is reachable with an indeterminate pointer.

findings.title: Server-controlled bv_len truncated from 64-bit into 32-bit string length
findings.invariant: The value length passed to the PHP string API must equal the actual number of bytes at bv_val; a ber_len_t must not be narrowed to a 32-bit length without a range check.
findings.failure_mode: bv_len is a ber_len_t (server-controlled, unsigned long / size_t width) while add_index_stringl's length parameter is 32-bit. On 64-bit builds a value whose bv_len > UINT_MAX is silently truncated, so the resulting PHP string's length disagrees with its backing buffer — producing value corruption or an inconsistent length used against the duplicated buffer. Requires a server returning an oversized attribute value.
findings.what_to_confirm: The exact width of add_index_stringl's length parameter versus ber_len_t on the target ABI, and whether bv_len is range-checked before the copy.

findings.title: ldap_get_values_len() return not explicitly NULL-checked
findings.code:
ldap_value = ldap_get_values_len(ldap, ldap_result_entry, attribute);
			num_values = ldap_count_values_len(ldap_value);
findings.invariant: A NULL return from ldap_get_values_len must not be dereferenced; safety must not depend on undocumented NULL-tolerance of ldap_count_values_len/ldap_value_free_len.
findings.failure_mode: ldap_get_values_len can return NULL for a value-less or malformed attribute. Here ldap_value is passed straight into ldap_count_values_len (line 49) and later ldap_value_free_len (line 57) without an explicit NULL check. Stock OpenLDAP treats NULL as count 0 / no-op (so the loop is skipped and no ldap_value[i] deref occurs), but on a library lacking that tolerance a server-supplied empty attribute yields a NULL dereference.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the linked libldap's ldap_count_values_len and ldap_value_free_len are documented NULL-safe; otherwise add an explicit 'if (ldap_value)' guard.

findings.title: ldap_get_dn() NULL return correctly guarded (regression anchor)
findings.anchor: dn = ldap_get_dn(...); if (dn) { add_assoc_string(tmp1, "dn", dn, 1); } else { add_assoc_null(tmp1, "dn"); }
findings.invariant: ldap_get_dn's return must be NULL-checked before being treated as a C string by add_assoc_string (which strlen()s it).
findings.failure_mode: ldap_get_dn returns NULL for an entry whose DN cannot be parsed. As written the 'if (dn)' guard stores a PHP null instead, so no dereference occurs — this path is SAFE. It is recorded because it is the canonical CWE-476 sink for this function: deleting the guard (the pre-fix state) re-introduces a remotely-triggerable NULL dereference in add_assoc_string.
findings.what_to_confirm: That the 'if (dn)' guard is retained around add_assoc_string on all code paths and versions of this function.

findings.title: Unbounded PHP array growth from server-controlled counts
findings.anchor: int num_entries, num_attrib, num_values, i; ... for (i = 0; i < num_values; i++)
findings.invariant: The counts come from real, allocated, NULL-terminated libldap lists so loop bounds cannot exceed the actual arrays; only total memory is unbounded and must be capped by PHP's memory_limit.
findings.failure_mode: num_values/num_attrib/num_entries are signed ints from ldap_count_*; they cannot exceed the genuine list lengths, so the loops stay in-bounds and a -1 error return is separately handled. The only residual risk is DoS: an LDAP server returning a very large number of entries/values forces large PHP array allocation and memory use.
findings.what_to_confirm: That no unbounded accumulation escapes PHP's memory_limit, and that ldap_count_* return values are never used as sizes elsewhere without the terminating-NULL invariant.

audit_checklist: Confirm ber is initialised to NULL (or ber_free is gated on ldap_first_attribute success) before line 70 can run.
Verify the width of add_index_stringl's length parameter vs ber_len_t and add a bv_len range check before the value copy at line 55.
Add or confirm an explicit NULL check on ldap_get_values_len's return (line 48) rather than relying on library NULL-tolerance.
Ensure the 'if (dn)' guard around add_assoc_string (lines 77-81) is preserved in every version/branch of ldap_get_entries.
Check that ldap_count_entries returning -1 cannot flow into any size/index arithmetic beyond the handled first_entry==NULL path.
Confirm no per-attribute/per-entry zval (tmp1/tmp2) leaks on early error paths, and that memory growth is bounded by PHP memory_limit for hostile large result sets.
